data.title: Chicago Atlantic BDC: Capturing The Growth Of Cannabis
data.description:
Overview

Chicago Atlantic BDC (NASDAQ:LIEN) is a business development company that generates its earnings from a portfolio of debt investments. What differentiates LIEN from peer BDCs is that a significant portion of its portfolio is concentrated on cannabis businesses. I previously covered LIEN and issued a buy rating due to the attractive valuation and strong potential for growth in the future. LIEN recently reported Q4 earnings, and I wanted to use this as an opportunity to provide some updated insights into the BDC amongst the current market turmoil.

Looking at the performance over the last twelve months, we can see that LIEN's price has increased by about 9.2%. When including all distributions that have been paid out to shareholders, the total return jumps up to about 20.6% over the same time frame. Following the recent price drop, LIEN has a generous starting dividend yield of 12.7%, which makes it a great option for investors seeking a source of reliable dividend income. Based on the most recent earnings, I believe the distribution can be supported, but there is definitely room for improvement.

LIEN still offers investors an attractive value proposition, as the price trades at a sizable discount to NAV. Additionally, there is plenty of growth potential within the cannabis industry as the market demand continues to grow year after year. LIEN went public in 2021, which makes it still unproven. There may be some cracks starting to show, related to the growing PIK income and lack of NAV growth. However, I remain optimistic about the outlook as the fundamentals are strong and management continues to increase the diversity of the portfolio. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying portfolio strategy implemented.

Portfolio Strategy

According to the most recent portfolio breakdown, LIEN has net assets amounting to $301M, that's spread across 28 different portfolio companies. This makes LIEN a lot more concentrated than some peer BDCs out there. For instance, TriplePoint Venture Growth (TPVG) is a BDC that has a portfolio of 128 different loans. 77% of LIEN's portfolio is allocated towards Cannabis businesses, with only 23% related to industries outside of Cannabis. This can potentially be a big source of vulnerability for LIEN since Cannabis isn't yet legal everywhere across the United States. There's always a risk that LIEN could be impacted by regulatory changes that can be a threat to the earnings potential.

To offset this risk, management has been trying to grow out the non-Cannabis portion of their portfolio. We can see that Finance and Insurance investments account for the largest non-Cannabis portion of the portfolio at 49%. This is followed by exposure to Information Tech and Public Administration investments, accounting for 23% and 16%, respectively. Management has ensured investors, on the last earnings call, that they apply the same level of quality underwriting when it comes to investments outside of Cannabis.

We have segmented these opportunities into three sub-strategies, which include growth in technology companies, esoteric and asset-based lending opportunities, and companies in need of liquidity-driven debt solutions. These three sub-strategies greatly expand the number of companies and industries that we can work with to tailor customized lending solutions, diversify the portfolio and enhance our ability to deliver differentiated credit alpha. Similar to how we have approached cannabis investing, we are focused on lending to leading companies, brands and management teams with low debt to enterprise value and ample collateral or cash flow coverage. We have the same methodical and quantitatively driven approach to underwriting.—Dino Colonna, President

80% of the portfolio operates on a floating rate basis, which has allowed LIEN to benefit from the higher interest rate environment. As interest rates remain near their decade-high, LIEN can continue to generate a higher amount of interest income from borrowers. A higher interest rate directly translates to a higher cost of borrowing for the companies within LIEN's portfolio. Due to stubbornly high inflationary data, the Fed has adjusted their outlook on interest rates and now only estimates there to be one cut to take place over 2025. There is still a lot of uncertainty in the air, though, especially with the ongoing market declines related to Trump's tariffs.

What does bring me a little reassurance is that 100% of the investments are structured as senior secured. Senior secured debt sits at the top of the corporate capital structure, which means that these investments have the highest priority for repayment. This can act as a defensive layer for LIEN since it reduces the probability that LIEN loses all invested capital in a bad transaction. If a borrower is struggling and defaults on their loan, they may need to liquidate assets. In this scenario, LIEN's debt would be entitled to repayment before any other forms of debt the borrower may have.

Updated Valuation and Risk Profile

LIEN is still new and unproven, which may be why the price continues to trade at a large discount to the NAV valuation. For instance, the price currently trades at a discount to NAV of approximately 19%. As markets are currently trending downwards, there is a possibility that the discount widens if the price continues to fall. Since LIEN launched during 2022's market decline, the price has traded at an average discount to NAV of 25.7% over the last three-year period. Despite this, Wall Street analysts seem to be optimistic based on their average price target of $13.28 per share. This would indicate a potential upside of over 24% from the current level.

The biggest driver of growth for LIEN would be the estimate's sales increase in the Cannabis market. When combining adult recreational sales and medical sales, the Cannabis market is estimated to increase at a CAGR (compound annual growth rate) of 10% through 2030. However, these estimates don't specify whether or not they take into account the possibility of additional states making Cannabis use 100% legal.

There are still many states in the US where Cannabis is fully illegal, and many states where recreational use is still not allowed. These estimates may actually be conservative if we consider the possibility that additional states can fully legalize the use of Cannabis at some point over the next decade, which would have a positive impact on the overall growth potential for LIEN.

However, LIEN will always be vulnerable to the risk of stricter policies being implemented to stop the use of Cannabis. The legalization of Cannabis will ultimately make or break the fund over the long term. If you are optimistic about the possibility of Cannabis eventually being legalized throughout the US, LIEN is a great play because the BDC still has great foundations.

For instance, LIEN still has no accruals as part of its portfolio, despite markets dealing with the elevated interest rates for a prolonged period of time. In comparison, I've frequently covered many other BDCs that have seen sizable upticks in the rate of non-accruals within their portfolio. Just to name some, here are the non-accrual rates for some peer BDCs:

  • Ares Capital (ARCC): 0.9% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • FS KKR Capital (FSK): 2.2% non-accrual rate at fair value.
  • BlackRock TCP Capital (TCPC): 5.6% non-accrual rate at fair value.

While non-accruals don't paint the entire picture, we can see how LIEN has outperformed these peers in total return over the last twelve months.

Despite LIEN not having any non-accruals, I think we may eventually see some over the course of 2025 due to the rising amount of PIK (payment-in-kind) income being received. An increasing PIK income is usually an indication that some underlying borrowers are struggling to pay their required debt maintenance. Instead of paying their debt with cash, LIEN may allow some underperforming borrowers to issue more equity or debt as a way to defer payments until conditions improve. PIK income was only $115K a year ago but has since increased to $743K, according to the latest 10-K Form.

Q4 Earnings

LIEN recently reported their Q4 earnings, and the results were solid. Most notably, net investment income amounted to $0.35 per share, and total investment income was $12.7M. This represents a total investment income growth of 245% year over year! Similarly, net investment income also saw an increase from the prior year's Q4 total of $0.27 per share.

On a fiscal year basis, LIEN experienced a total net investment income of $9.5M. This was a sizable year-over-year increase from the prior year's total net investment income of $6.6M. The growth in income is a clear indication that management's strategy is effective and generates positive results for shareholders. Net Assets at the end of prior year's Q4 only amounted to $85.6M, but this has now increased to $301.2M. Despite all of these increases, LIEN's NAV per share has ultimately declined for several consecutive quarters now. For instance, here are the year-ending NAV amounts:

  • Q4 2023 NAV: $13.77 per share.
  • Q4 2024 NAV: $13.20 per share.

I believe the stunted NAV growth can be related to rising expenses related to the prior acquisition that took place. Even when excluding the acquisition expenses, regular expenses increased up to $8.3M for the quarter and more than doubled on a year-over-year basis up to $14.8M. Once management gets these expenses under control, I believe that LIEN can see impressive NAV growth going forward since the underlying fundamentals are strong. LIEN has $23.9M in cash and equivalents and no debt, according to management.

At the BDC level, we had no debt as of year-end as we continued to deploy cash from the balance sheet to fund new investments. Subsequent to year-end, we closed on the new $100 million credit facility.—Martin Rodgers, Chief Financial Officer

Dividend

As of the latest declared quarterly distribution of $0.34 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 12.7%. As previously mentioned, net investment income landed at $0.35 per share. This indicates that the distribution was supported by earnings, but there is definitely room for some improvement since earnings essentially cover the distribution with a coverage rate of only 103%. Any negative shifts in the environment can result in the distribution being reduced. As mentioned, non-accruals are low, but PIK income has significantly increased. For reference, the dividend coverage was a bit stronger at 114.7% at the time of my prior coverage.

For these reasons, I wouldn't expect any sort of underlying dividend raise to happen anytime soon. Additionally, I have no expectations for a supplemental distribution to be announced in the short term. However, LIEN did manage to fund $24.8M worth of new investments during Q4. Once these investments start to materialize into higher earnings, we can eventually see distribution coverage strengthen.

However, it's worth mentioning that the distributions received from LIEN are classified as ordinary dividends. Ordinary dividends have the least favorable tax consequences for investors compared to the qualified dividends you may receive from more traditional dividend-paying stocks. Based on your specific income bracket, amount of capital invested, and desired income level, a position in LIEN may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account to offset any potential tax burdens.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I maintain my buy rating on LIEN as the dividend is still supported, the price trades at an attractive discount to NAV valuation, and there is a ton of growth potential within the Cannabis market that can be a catalyst. The underlying fundamentals are strong as the portfolio strategy has proven to be effective. This is supported by a growing net investment income year over year, alongside no non-accruals. However, there are sources of weakness that could limit the appeal. For instance, net PIK income has significantly increased, and NAV growth has been disappointing over the last year. However, I believe the pros outweigh the risks at this moment.

data.title: PTY Vs. PDI: This Is The Wrong Time For PDI
data.description:
PTY and PDI CEF: yield curve inversion

I last analyzed the PIMCO Corporate and Income Opportunity Fund (NYSE:PTY) back in Feb 2025. That article was titled “PDO Vs. PTY: This Chart Shows Why I Now Prefer PDO”. As the title hinted, that analysis compared PTY to its PDO with a focus on their relative yield spread. The article ended with a hold rating for PTY based on the following considerations:

The thick yield spread between PDO and PTY suggests PDO’s wide margin of safety, further augmented by its much lower price premium. I am also more optimistic about PDO's mortgage-related assets and feel quite pessimistic about PTY's high-yield credit exposure.

As for the PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund (NYSE:PDI), I last covered it back in Aug 2024. My last article, titled “PDI: Much Better Return/Risk Ratio Thanks To Rate Cut Outlook (Upgrade)”, recommended a hold rating for this CEF with the following considerations:

Earlier, I argued it was among the worst times to invest in non-investment grade debts, like those held in PIMCO Dynamic Income Fund in 3 decades. Now, I see an improved return/risk profile with decreased interest rates and widened yield spread. Looking ahead, the likelihood of multiple interest rate cuts by 2024 should help to further add to the skewness.

Since those writings, there have been quite a few fundamental changes in the overall economic and geopolitical landscape. The remainder of this article will focus on the most critical one for PTY and PDI in my view: the inversion of the yield. To wit, the chart below displays the spread between 10-Year Treasury Rates and 3-Month Treasury Rates (T10Y3M) since April 2020. As seen, the spread has dipped below zero since my last writing and remained negative as of this writing. The current spread is -0.27%, indicating an inverted yield curve. An inverted yield curve has been a leading indicator of multiple recessions in the past, which can pressure both PDI and PTY in the negative direction. However, in the remainder of this article, I will explain why the negative impact would be uneven. To be more specific, I expect the inversion of the yield curve to pressure PDI more severely than PTY because of PDI’s larger exposure (more than 55% when adjusted for duration) to mortgage-related assets and its more aggressive leverage.

These considerations have led me to downgrade my rating on PDI to sell under the current conditions and reiterate my hold rating on PTY.

PDI and PTY CEF: basic information

To prime the more in-depth examination, let me first introduce PDI and PTY briefly, with a special focus on their exposure. Both funds are taxable bond funds managed by the PIMCO Corporate. They share several fundamental strategies as detailed from their fund descriptions (quoted below from their webpage). The parts that are most relevant to this analysis were highlighted by me (which is their exposure to mortgage-related and other asset-backed securities). I will revisit and elaborate on this later.

PTY uses a dynamic asset allocation strategy that focuses on duration management, credit quality analysis, risk management techniques, and broad diversification among issuers, industries, and sectors. Under normal market conditions, the Fund seeks to invest at least 80% of its net assets plus borrowings for investment purposes in a combination of corporate debt obligations of varying maturities, other corporate income-producing securities, and income-producing securities of non-corporate issuers, such as U.S. Government securities, municipal securities and mortgage-backed and other asset-backed securities issued on a public or private basis.

PDI seeks current income as a primary objective and capital appreciation as a secondary objective. The Fund seeks to achieve its investment objectives by utilizing a dynamic asset allocation strategy among multiple fixed income sectors in the global credit markets, including corporate debt, mortgage-related and other asset-backed securities, government and sovereign debt, taxable municipal bonds, and other fixed-, variable- and floating-rate income-producing securities of U.S. and foreign issuers, including emerging market issuers.

The two charts below show a few other notable features of these funds. As seen in the first chart, PTY has a much longer history and was created in 2002. It has an expense ratio of 1.19%. PDI is the newer fund, created in 2012, and charges a higher expense ratio of 2.18%. Despite PDI’s shorter history, it has accumulated a significantly larger AUM of $6.17 billion, compared to PTY’s $2.09 billion. PDI’s popularity is for good reasons. To start, PDI offers a much higher dividend yield than PTY, which is a key draw for many investors who come to PIMCO in the first place. To wit, PDI’s currently yields 14.91%. In contrast, PTY's current yield is “only” 10.97%. In the past 4 years, PDI’s average yield has been about 13.7%, vs. about 10% for PTY. PDI also uses leverage more aggressively (more on this later), which helped to augment its performance in good times. And the past 10 years or so since PDI’s inception were indeed good times in my view, characterized by extremely low borrowing rates and one of the best bull runs.

But with the inversion of the yield curve, I think things are about to change, as detailed next.

PDI and PTY CEF: A closer look at exposure

I opened the article with the inversion of the yield curve for two reasons: 1) its role as a leading indicator of a recession in the past, and 2) its impact on mortgage-related assets. For readers unfamiliar with the second reason, the long-short yield spread is a key factor for the health of these assets because of the following dynamics described in my earlier article. These assets tend to be valued more favorably when the short-term rates are above the long-term rates, as they borrow at short-term rates to profit in the longer term. With the short-long yield spread turning negative, I thus expect the opposite going forward.

And unfortunately, mortgage-related assets are precisely the largest exposure for PDI. To be more explicit, the chart below displays the sector allocation percentages for PTY and PDI. As seen, PTY's largest allocation by market value (MV%) is to High Yield Credit at 30.52%, followed by Emerging Markets at 15.25%. Non-Agency Mortgage takes the 3rd place with a relatively minor weight of 13.86%. In contrast, PDI's top allocation is to Non-Agency Mortgage at 25.21%. The exposure is even more concerning in my view in terms of Duration Weighted Exposure (DWE%). For PTY, Non-Agency Mortgage still is its 3rd exposure in DWE, but at a higher level of 19.8% as seen. For PDI, the DWE of Non-Agency Mortgage is more than 55%, making this its largest exposure by far.

Other risks and final thoughts

Before closing, a few other risks for both funds are worth mentioning. As aforementioned, PDI uses leverage more aggressively, which can be a double-edged sword. The chart below details the leverage positions for both PDI and PTY. As seen, PDI's total debt is last reported at $2.839 billion, resulting in an effective leverage ratio of 31.53%. In contrast, PTY shows a significantly lower effective leverage ratio of 9.67%. Given the uncertainties ahead and the relatively elevated borrowing rates, I think this is the wrong time to apply aggressive leverage.

Of course, PDI offers quite a few positives too, besides its much higher yield as mentioned earlier. As you can see from the following chart, PDI holds a significantly larger number of individual assets (as many as 1900) than PTY (about 836) and thus offers greater diversification. PDI also has a slightly lower concentration in its top holdings (with 32.56% of its assets allocated to its top 10) than PTY's 34.83%, thus offering lower concentration risks. PTY also exhibits a higher turnover rate of 31%, almost double that of PDI's 17%. A higher turnover rate tends to correlate with more trading costs and larger tax headwinds.

More importantly, PDI has demonstrated a lower correlation to the overall equity market (as approximated by the SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust, SPY in the table below) in the past. To wit, PTY and PDI have exhibited a relatively strong positive correlation of 0.78 against each other due to the overlap of their strategy. PTY also exhibited a quite strong positive correlation of 0.71 with SPY, while PDI’s correlation is noticeably lower at 0.66. Thus, for investors whose portfolios have substantial exposure to the SP500 (I suppose most/many of us do), PDI’s lower correlation could better help stabilize the overall portfolio.

All told, my verdict is that the downside risks entailed by PDI far outweigh the positives under current conditions. To recap, the leading risk in my view is the inversion of the yield curve – a risk more concerning for PDI than PTY, both because of PDI’s larger exposure to mortgage-related assets and also its heavier leverage in my model. Due to these considerations, this article downgrades my PDI rating to sell, and I recommend income investors consider PTY instead. Despite PTY’s lower yield, I have a more positive view of its total return potential and reiterate my hold rating.

data.title: Southwest Airlines: A Stalling Airline For First Class Prices
data.description:
It has been turbulent for airline stocks over the past few weeks thanks to the reduced US domestic outlook on passenger foot traffic and economic headwinds from the looming US recession. However, I think investors are missing key signs from the airlines still punching above their weight, but Southwest Airlines Co. (NYSE:LUV) could be missing out on a potential tailwind.

What are Southwest Airlines' operations like?

Southwest Airlines is one of the biggest Airlines in the world, operating 801 aircraft in the US domestic budget sector. Southwest Airlines only offers economy class and does not offer business class or first-class tickets. This exposes them to more internal macroeconomic turmoil within the US than its competitors due to recession fears and expected economic hardship from incoming tariffs hitting their passenger base harder. In fact, the North American routes are the only region to experience a YoY decline in Revenue Passenger Kilometers (RPK).

On top of this, domestic flight RPK has shrunk YoY bucking an over 12-month run of form and lagging behind its international counterparts. Meaning Southwest has the worst of both worlds, a shrinking market and, with the majority of their income coming from economy class, the most likely to be affected by this recession and cut spending.

As shown here North America has bucked the global trend of passenger load ratios as well, meaning reduced efficiency as more seats are left empty on flights, reducing potential profits. Passenger load factor = RPKs / Available Seat Kilometers (ASKs).

The best way to assess Southwest Airlines, however, is to look at its financial ratios and efficiency ratios from FY2024 against the domestic market and global average, as seen in my airline market snapshot:

Airline United Airlines Holdings, Inc. (UAL) Delta Air Lines, Inc. (DAL) American Airlines Group Inc. (AAL) Southwest Airlines Co. (LUV) Industry Average, U.S. Global Jets ETF (JETS)
PE 5.42 5.92 5.18 17.73 12.44
PEG 0.45 0.2 0.35 0.5 0.33
EV/FCF 10.9 10.5 28.15 -12.82 8.96
Operating Revenue Per ATK ($) 1.01 1.06 1.02 0.95 0.59
Load Ratio (%) 83 84 85 80 83
Fleet Size 1406 983 985 801 615

The unfortunate truth for Southwest is that they are simply not cutting it compared to their major US counterparts (except the likes of the bankrupt Spirit Airways, and more local operators like SkyWest, Inc. (SKYW)). They became comfortable in a growing market and did not focus on efficiency enough to build an FCF margin to cover their operations. As such they underperform in every single metric against the industry average except RATK, showing their ability to profit from their available aircraft at about 95c when they move 1 tonne of passengers and freight by 1 kilometer, reflecting their relatively efficient operating model against global airlines.

For Southwest, this is a promising sign for their ability to produce revenue from their seats, but despite an advantage in the global market, as RPK and load factor drop in the US domestic market over the coming months, Southwest is going to see a significant drawdown on that ratio. Change in load factor is proportional to change in RATK, so seeing a significant drop in load factor would put Southwest's already tenuously competitive profit margin at risk. Also, Southwest's domestic competitors far outperform them efficiency-wise whilst having international routes and business class.

Valuation gets no better for Southwest. With an EV/FCF of -12.82, they simply don't have the cash flow to compete and take market share from the passengers that remain flying through this macroeconomic turmoil. On top of this, they have a forward P/E far higher than the industry average and a high PEG ratio as well. These are all pointers that Southwest's current position holding their operating margins this high has propped up their share price and now overvaluation is almost a certainty.

How could they improve?

Short of completely changing their operating model, there is nothing they can do to avoid this macroeconomic headwind now. During the strong year last year, it would have been beneficial for them to focus on business profitability to extract a positive FCF to provide a margin for their own solvency. Their debt-to-equity ratio is lowering, however.

One thing to note is that from FY2021 until FY2024, Southwest's EPS decreased year over year. It has not recovered as much profit as many other airlines since COVID. Therefore, if they can weather this turmoil, it might be wise to keep an eye on their profitability, as Fwd earnings growth looks promising in the 2025 outlook despite market fears.

What is their value?

We will start by listing Southwest's market ratios against the industry averages taken from the IATA and Seeking Alpha:

Metric GAAP non-GAAP Industry GAAP Industry non-GAAP
Fwd P/E 19.1 17.75 12.44 12.44
Expected EPS 1.66 1.77 - -
PEG -73.46153846 -2.876823339 1 1
EPS growth rate -0.26 -6.17 8.2 8.2
Fair Value 31.706 31.4175 - -
Fwd P/S 0.64 - 0.5 -

Let's calculate the cost needed to justify owning this stock:

ERP = Predicted market YoY growth - Current federal bond yield = 8.49% - 4.37% = 4.12%.

Cost of Equity = Current federal bond yield + (Beta x ERP) = 4.37% + (0.99 x 4.12%) = 8.42%.

We can use this to perform 5-year back-projection DCF modeling with Wall St analyst predictions, varying the model and terminal growth to obtain a range of upsides from the current share price.

Terminal Growth -3% 0% +3%
FCFF upside (%) -22.35109718 -15.51724138 70.57053292
FCFF w/out Capex upside (%) 23.51097179 45.10971787 176.8777429

This gives us a total upside spread as follows:

We can plot these in distributions to work out how likely it will be for the stock to justify a purchase by outperforming CoE, let's start by finding the mean and standard deviation:

Nμ = Σ(upsides) / n = -0.017.

Nσ2 = Σ(xi - Nμ)2/n.

Nσ2 = 5198.41.

Nσ = √5198.41 = 72.10.

μ = Ln(Nμ2/√(Nμ2 + Nσ2)) = -12.35948938.

σ = √(Ln(1 + (Nσ2/Nμ2))) = 4.078915147.

Returning the distribution curves below:

This gives us an average probability of returning above CoE of:

(33.4+0.0001)/2 = 16.7%.

This data is certainly skewed, looking at the log-normal graph. Not just by the fat-tailed property of securities, but that the log-normal mean is negative. This gives a very poor return, but is supported by the normal curve which is less representative of market conditions, but will have less extremely skewed results due to negatives.

By performing a Monte Carlo simulation, we improve statistical reliability. We have used the same financial ratios as earlier and DCF modeling, using the GAAP and non-GAAP values as the spread on each value +/- σ between the two. DCF models will be taken as a range of 6 values with their own μ and σ, varying by μ +/- σ. The random spread will be modeled using a log-normal distribution to represent the fat-tailed properties of securities.

This graph shows a huge uncertainty of over 11% between the most and least profitable simulations, which is almost one empirical standard deviation. This shows a very weak valuation as compared to our previous articles where we have seen "Hold" stocks have a 7% spread and "Strong Buy" stocks have a 4% spread. On top of this, every single mean of the simulation returns under CoE, with the probability of returning above CoE being under 70%, but above half, this demonstrates the property of securities to outweigh their mean distributions.

We can take a mean of our distributions and our simulation to obtain a probability of returning above CoE of 32.46%, which is plotted on our empirical ratings curve (Normal, μ =50, σ = 12.5), returns us a "Sell" rating:

We can arrive at an appropriate PT by multiplying our mean upsides by our average P(x > CoE) to obtain a risk-adjusted PT. This considers all models used and gives us a reliable target price.

$25.10 (Current share price) * ((-0.017*0.3245)+1) = $24.95.

What is your rating?

I rate Southwest Airlines a "Sell" with a PT of $24.95 and a 32.5% chance of outperforming Cost of Equity (8.42%).

This reflects the poor market outlook for domestic economy airlines not just in the US but globally, exacerbated by tariff fears, traffic decline, load factor deterioration, US market relative underperformance, and Southwest's overvaluation and reduced efficiency against market peers, despite efficient fleet operations.

On top of this, Southwest's low FCF will put extra strain on the solvency of the business, as load factor and revenues may be set to decline.

data.title: Oakmark Fixed Income Market Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Today’s uncertainty gets priced into the market

Headlines don’t reflect the whole story

One day we’re told tariffs are “coming back stronger than ever.” The next, negotiations may “ease tensions for good.” By Friday, it’s back to “global realignment.” The latest? Widespread, large tariffs are expected to be implemented by our president. Reading these daily macro alerts feels like trying to interpret a seismograph during one of my daughter’s bounce house birthday parties.

Sure, headline-driven chaos suggests uncertainty is rising—and maybe it is—but probably not as much as it feels. Uncertainty is always in the background; it’s a structural feature of the future. What actually changes more often is how much uncertainty people focus on—and whether market asset prices reflect that. What matters most to us at Oakmark isn’t whether uncertainty exists—it always does—but whether market asset prices reflect uncertainty in a way that creates a sufficient value opportunity. Until recently, they didn’t. Most of the credit world was priced as if companies’ future earnings power carried no uncertainty—not just around tariffs, but broadly. But in the final month of this quarter, something changed: markets began to price in uncertainty. And that’s good news for those of us who’ve been waiting to find value again after credit’s dry spell over the past couple of years.

After an extended stretch of rising equity markets and declining credit risk, prices are finally starting to move in the other direction. Only recently have we started to see signs of opportunity returning. At Oakmark, the team has leaned defensive for the better part of two years, preferring higher quality corporate balance sheets, securitization vehicles, and plain old U.S. governments. There was no need to chase yield or make big bets on lower quality companies that offered minimal compensation for a significant drop in credit quality—especially when we were finding better expected returns in higher quality balance sheets. That calculus is shifting as we exit the quarter and prices move even lower. We’re beginning to put capital to work where risk premiums are finally starting to reflect reality. I’m sharpening the pencil, and for the first time in a while, we’re starting to draw.

Value begins to return

The repricing hasn’t been dramatic, but it matters. It’s how value in credit starts to rebuild. High yield (Bloomberg U.S. Corporate High Yield Index) has widened to +375 as of writing—almost 100 basis points off the tights (+281 on February 18, 2025) or a move of roughly 33%. Investment-grade BBB credit (Bloomberg U.S. BBB Corporate Index)—where most investment grade issuance sits—has widened to around +120, 25 basis points wider and 27% off the lows. Since the global financial crisis, spreads for high yields have ranged between 300–650 basis points, and spreads for investment grade have ranged between100–200 for investment grade 84% of the time since March 20101. So, the recent shifts represent meaningful resets. They have the potential to generate not just total return, but excess return for long-term, patient capital.

In parts of high yield and BBB investment grade, spreads are finally starting to reflect real risks: business model fragility, capital structure leverage, refinancing gaps. Securitized credit is repricing too, especially in deals tied to consumer credit and commercial real estate where stress is more visible. These aren’t broad calls. We believe this market still rewards real credit work, not index exposure. But for the first time in a while, dislocations are surfacing that meet Oakmark’s bar for both return potential and margin of safety.

Finding opportunity in dislocations

One such dislocation emerged in auto asset-backed securities (auto ABS). We bought a newly issued BBB-rated tranche at a credit spread of 190 basis points, almost double what the market would have priced that level of risk even three weeks earlier. Such a large move was purely technical and disconnected from our view of the investment’s fundamental risk. In fact, the underlying auto loans would have to experience cumulative losses above 40% for our investment to see any write-down, while that type of loan pool typically exhibits losses below 20%. In our opinion, that healthy margin of safety against losses, combined with a rapid cheapening of credit spreads, created the kind of opportunity that fits our value investment thesis.

We also added exposure in Insulet Corp. (PODD) by participating in its inaugural senior unsecured bond deal used to refinance part of its 2026 convertible notes. This marked a meaningful evolution in PODD’s capital structure—from a growth-phase mix of converts and loans to something more befitting its scale and cash flow profile. We see it as a high-BB credit, anchored by a market leadership position in insulin delivery, strong free cash flow, and modest, declining leverage near 2.5x. Price talk came in at 7%, roughly 50 basis points wide of comparable high-quality healthcare credits–and 50 basis points wider than it would have been priced earlier in March before valuations in credit began to reset. That kind of mispricing doesn’t just offer near-term yield. It sets up longer-term potential upside as our upgrade thesis plays out. These are still selective opportunities, not a broad-based buying spree. But the key difference is that we’re finally being paid to take risk. After a long period of restraint, we believe it’s time to start leaning in—carefully, selectively, and always with our bottom-up framework in mind.

Still early days

My goal isn’t to signal the “all-in” on buying fixed income risk today. If tariffs of the magnitude that have been recently discussed are fully enacted and sustained, we believe there will be plenty of opportunities to buy at even lower prices than today, especially in more cyclical corners of the marketplace. Credit markets today aren’t broadly cheap, but they’re far more interesting than they were just a quarter ago. Spread widening—especially in companies with reliable management teams and revenue streams—has opened isolated entry points that finally offer fair compensation for real risk. The reset likely isn’t complete and may even be in the early stages, but it’s in motion.

Our positioning today

With that in mind, our portfolio positioning reflects a cautious optimism. We’ve begun selectively increasing exposure to credit risk in areas where spreads offer meaningful compensation. That includes certain segments of high yield, BBB-rated corporates, and asset-backed securities with conservative structures and favorable technicals. At the same time, we’ve maintained strong liquidity and continue to hold a healthy allocation of U.S. Treasurys and short-duration instruments—both for defense and optionality. The opportunity set is growing, but patience remains a core part of our process as we assess further possible disruptions in our companies’ earnings streams.

How much disruption—and ultimately what gets priced into the market—will depend in large part on macro policy. At Oakmark, we don’t try to predict how these events will play out. We leave that to the few PhDs who claim they can. But we don’t ignore it either. Tariffs are real. They have the potential to shift earnings and raise default risk.

We do spend time focusing on how the companies in our portfolio can absorb that reset—through cash flow, balance sheet strength, and liquidity. That’s the key distinction. As debt investors, we don’t need to predict the exact outcome. But we do need a strong sense of the range—especially the downside—and the catalysts that could take us there.

Rather than betting on discrete macro outcomes, we focus on building portfolios of companies led by management teams equipped to navigate a wide range of scenarios. While others try to forecast the next macro squall, hoping to steer around it, we’re doing something different: picking what we believe are the right captain and the right boat. We want leadership that has proven it can navigate calm seas and rough waters alike and businesses that are built to absorb volatility, not avoid it. If we trust the captain and know the vessel is sound, we don’t need to predict every storm. We just need to stay on course.

That means we position our portfolio to hold up in tightening or easing cycles, recession or recovery, inflation or deflation. And we focus on securities where we think the embedded risk premium compensates us for real uncertainty—not headlines. When dislocations emerge, we lean in where the downside is well understood, and the upside still reflects investor fear. That mindset kept us disciplined during years of aggressive pricing, and we believe it will now help us step back into a market that looks more compelling.

In short, the market feels different—not because uncertainty is new, but because it’s finally being acknowledged and priced. Tariffs may be the catalyst, and we are monitoring their effects on the market. But what is notable is the shift in sentiment from complacency to caution, from greed to fear. And that shift creates opportunity for disciplined value buyers.

Through it all, our process doesn’t change. We stay grounded in fundamentals. We don’t react to noise. We invest in management teams and capital structures built to handle a range of outcomes. And when we find mispriced risk with a wide margin of safety, we act.

Others try to time the next macro storm. We focus on understanding the range of weather we could face and on making sure we have the right captain and the right boat to get through it. That mindset—clear-eyed, bottom-up, forward-leaning—has always defined Oakmark. And right now, it has us exactly where we want to be: sailing toward the next opportunity for our investors, confident in the captains we’ve chosen, steady in the boat, and unfazed by the wind.

Adam D. Abbas

Portfolio Manager

data.title: Verizon: Still A Worthwhile Dividend Play
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman.

Verizon Communications Inc. (NYSE:VZ) has seen its share price climb as we've been going into 2025, up over 6%. That was over 10% at one point before the latest market panic selling kicked off on the back of President Trump's trade war. That's also much better than what the broader market has been seeing, with the SPDR® S&P 500® ETF Trust (SPY) down nearly 14%.

Even further for VZ more specifically, it was after the Chief Revenue Officer mentioned in a recent conference that year-over-year Q1 numbers would be tough; also, he had mentioned that there was a "headwind on churn" early in 2025. That saw shares fall quite dramatically but have slowly recovered some losses seen after those remarks.

VZ, as most folks probably know, competes primarily with T-Mobile US, Inc. (TMUS) and AT&T Inc. (T) as the largest and main competitors. While this is a bit of an oligopoly, competition is still fierce in the space, and that can limit growth potential.

Instead, VZ will not be considered something worth expecting a ton of appreciation for over the long run. However, it's this boring nature of the business and predictable cash flow that can make it particularly attractive for its dividend.

Rates Likely Heading Lower Benefits VZ

This dividend is also what can make VZ quite interest rate sensitive as it is considered primarily an income investment. When rates are rising, investors can invest in Treasuries with competitive yields without the risk.

Since our last update, VZ has shown a strong total return, partially driven by lower Treasury Rates and even the Fed easing their benchmark rates.

A rising share price for VZ means a lower yield, as the dividend increases aren't anything to boast about. However, with a ~6.4% yield, we are still looking at a solid dividend.

VZ is also sensitive to the Fed rate due to its substantial amount of debt. As a quite recent example, VZ announced recently they'd be redeeming nearly a billion dollars worth of notes that had a coupon of 2.625%. That's certainly not something they'd be able to replace currently with the same rate with the United States 10-Year Bond Yield (US10Y) at around 4.3%.

There is good reason to believe that rates could continue to head lower based on the potential for the Trump administration to ramp up tariffs in round two of the trade wars since he's been elected.

As always, I like to convey that I'm not choosing any side here when it comes to politics. This is simply discussing what is happening and how it can impact an investment in VZ.

With that said, the tariffs are causing significant uncertainty. The ramping up and then adding leniency and then delaying constantly isn't helpful to companies trying to operate. Businesses can't make the sort of investments they may otherwise have without knowing the rules of the game.

Similarly, tariffs will put pressure on consumers as costs rise. It may be "transitory" costs as they get digested, meaning it is more of a one-time hit to the wallet, but it will be disruptive nonetheless. Of course, we then know when this occurs, recessions occur and investors can try to flock to safety, pushing Treasury Rates lower.

During this time, the Fed will be pressured to cut rates based on increasing unemployment. Not only is their mandate to try to keep inflation at their target, but they also have the mandate of maintaining a healthy employment level. That part of the equation doesn't always seem to be factored in when it has been inflation that has been front and center for several years now.

With the latest FOMC meeting, they reiterated that they continue to expect to cut rates by another 50 basis points in 2025.

Similarly, the market is projecting that there is a nearly 97% probability that there will be some easing by the end of this year. The highest by a thin margin is the 375-400 basis point range, but 350-375 is right there as well—indicating that there is a nearly 50% probability that we get 50 basis points of cuts or even more.

That said, the caveat for this is that it is always subject to change.

TMUS Better Growth Prospects And With Yield

Speaking of rates and dividends. This could also bring up another interesting point about TMUS—with TMUS initiating a dividend last year and with higher growth expectations, this could have been pulling some focus from investors as well. So, while VZ has been performing well over the last year now, it could have possibly been even better.

TMUS looks like a more appealing telecom giant now with better growth, and that's translating into a dividend that is likely to grow substantially faster. Analysts expect TMUS to grow at an average rate of 15.2% over the next five years compared to VZ's estimates of 3.65%.

So the question could become, why not just invest in them instead? That would be because it is also priced about 2.6x higher based on a forward P/E ratio. I could certainly see TMUS being a worthwhile investment, but there is greater risk there if they don't meet their targets and that multiple comes down.

Looking more specifically at VZ, I believe that Verizon is still a worthwhile investment for a dividend-focused investor. Based on its historical fair value range, we are still looking at trading right on the bottom end.

Options To Bring In Cash Flow

One way we've been playing Verizon is through options. This is another consideration for these sorts of boring dividend payers to help juice the "income." For VZ, I have a core long position, so this is being implemented on top of that.

The current outstanding options trade we have are the puts we sold with a strike price of $43. We collected $0.66 and initiated that trade on March 28, 2025; this was a roll-up and out of our previous short put option position.

Here's a breakdown of some of our previous trades going back to 2023. This highlights one of the full cycles of the option wheel strategy at play.

Ticker Expiration Date Upper Strike Current Price Type Sold Date Initiated Premium Collected Date Closed Closing Cost Gain/Loss
VZ 03/28/2025 $39.00 Rolled Puts 02/11/2025 $0.48 03/28/2025 $0.02 $0.46
VZ 12/20/2024 $40.00 Rolled Puts 10/24/2024 $0.45 12/18/2024 $0.09 $0.36
VZ 10/18/2024 $42.00 Assigned Calls 08/09/2024 $0.74 Assigned - $0.74
VZ 08/16/2024 $41.00 Rolled Calls 07/08/2024 $1.12 08/09/2024 $0.22 $0.90
VZ 07/19/2024 $41.00 Rolled Calls 03/25/2024 $1.53 07/08/2024 $0.54 $0.99
VZ 04/19/2024 $36.00 Rolled Calls 01/02/2024 $3.22 03/25/2024 $4.78 -$1.56
VZ 01/19/2024 $36.00 Rolled Calls 10/09/2023 $0.20 01/02/2024 $2.74 -$2.54
VZ 10/20/2023 $36.00 Assigned Puts 07/17/2023 $3.11 Assigned - $3.11
VZ 07/21/2023 $36.00 Rolled Puts 06/20/2023 $1.11 07/17/2023 $2.51 -$1.40
VZ 06/23/2023 $36.00 Rolled Puts 05/22/2023 $0.69 06/20/2023 $0.31 $0.38
VZ 05/26/2023 $36.00 Rolled Puts 04/25/2023 $0.37 04/25/2023 $0.29 $0.08

In this case, we had shares get assigned to us on the cheap after rolling the puts out several times. Ultimately, we took an early assignment on this position on October 9, 2023, shortly after the ex-dividend date for that quarter, as is often the case when trading in-the-money.

We then ended up having to roll the trade up and out on the covered call side. That took a lot of the premium, and we ultimately only collected $0.70 per contract over more than a year. That said, it gave us more of that upside participation as we also collected $6 in capital gains. While holding this batch of shares long, we further received $1.995 in three dividends.

This is something that happens, and it isn't necessarily great when it does occur as it highlights getting capped out on some gains—that is one of the major "flaws" of covered calls. However, like all investing, there are pros and cons.

Conclusion

VZ remains a rather attractive name for dividend investors despite its share price rising quite rapidly this year. The reason seems quite clear: lower rates are going to benefit a company like VZ, easing debt concerns—and, if Treasury Rates continue to ease, push more income-focused investors into riskier areas on the hunt for yield.

data.title: Vistek: Vistek's IPO Is A Bet On Big-League Growth
data.description:
Chasing the L6 Dream

When you think of companies powering Singapore's infrastructure backbone, you might think of Tai Sin Electric Limited or maybe Keystone Cable. But now, there's a new name about to hit the wires-literally.

Vistek (VTEK) is getting ready to go public-and there's a good reason you probably haven't heard of it until now.

It's a company that does cabling work for the telecom industry and handles energy infrastructure projects. They've got something called a CR-07 workhead license at contractor level L5.

Yeah… if that sounded like alphabet soup to you, don't worry. I had to read it twice too.

Breaking down the technical jargon, companies with an L5 classification can bid on public sector contracts worth up to S$16 million - that's around $11.8 million, assuming a conversion rate of $0.75 for every Singapore dollar.

All the companies I mentioned above have the L6 classification. This gives them an unlimited contract value to undertake public sector projects.

And that brings us to the first reason behind the IPO: Vistek wants to level up to L6-which would let them compete in the big leagues.

But how would the IPO help with this?

The Building and Construction Authority-the regulatory body that handles this in Singapore-establishes some guidelines for companies that want to apply for L6.

Among them, we can mention:

  1. A high capitalization and a working capital large enough to handle a ton of receivables. As you may know, in this industry, companies don't get paid upfront. They only receive payment once the project is done. On top of that, Vistek often has to front cash to contractors as a guarantee that the job will get done. In financial terms, that puts a real strain on cash flow. And when that happens, there's really only one fix: more working capital;
  2. History of successful large projects;
  3. Minimum number of professionals or technicians with specific qualifications;
  4. Clean work safety record.

With the money raised by the IPO, Vistek would kill two birds with one stone: it would invest part of it in the business while maintaining a cushion to deal with the increase in receivables from contractors.

Vistek has already said how it will use this raised capital:

  1. Expand and renew fleet of equipment and machinery;
  2. Seek expansion opportunities through M&A;
  3. Acquisition of storage facilities and construction of dormitories for foreign workers;
  4. Working capital.

This should be more than enough to reach the long-awaited L6.

The goal? To grab a bigger piece of the pie in Singapore.

Obviously, since Vistek operates through public tenders and contracts, there's always the risk of not being selected. And even when it does win, these contracts aren't recurring-so the company is always back at square one, bidding for the next job.

Some Colors About Market Size

But is the Energy and Telecommunications Cables segment big enough to deliver the kind of growth that gets us excited-or at least makes this IPO make sense?

Well, time to put on our forecasting hats.

Since there's no dedicated research on this niche, we'll have to work with a few educated guesses I'll lay out for you here.

If the Asia-Pacific wire and cable market is worth just over $88.3 billion, it's not a stretch to guess that Singapore makes up somewhere between 1.3% and 1.5% of that.

Why is that creaking?

Because Singapore's share of regional GDP usually falls around there-and given its strong government investment in infrastructure, it probably deserves a bit of a premium.

That puts the local market at roughly $1.3 to $1.4 billion.

With $16.71 million in revenue, Vistek holds just a small slice of the pie-about 1.22% of the estimated market. That makes sense, considering they're still operating under an L5 license, which limits the size of the contracts they can go after.

But how fast is the market expected to grow?

If we assume that it is expected to grow at a CAGR of at least 8% in ASEAN, we can expect slightly faster growth for Singapore. Perhaps low double digits year-on-year? I think so.

In recent years, several major projects in this space have been completed by a range of companies. Vistek, of course, has been steadily nibbling at the edges, taking on more local and mid-sized contracts.

But when you look at the L6-level contracts, that's where the heavy hitters come in-we're talking massive projects like the Underground Transmission Cable Tunnel, completed in 2019. That one involved 1,200 km of extra-high voltage cables and now supplies about 20% of Singapore's peak electricity demand.

More recently, there's the Sun Cable Project on the table-an ambitious $13 billion plan to import 1.75 GW of solar power from Australia via a 4,300 km undersea cable.

So yes. Investments should continue to grow at a higher annual rate than in ASEAN.

In any case, Vistek could gain exposure to emerging countries in the region through a joint venture (as they have already suggested in their IPO prospectus).

The Numbers Don't Disappoint

Despite its non-recurring sales, Vistek is showing encouraging growth.

This is quite different from other companies I am covering in ASEAN IPOs, such as BUUU Group (BUUU) and OFAL Group (OFAL).

Sales were $16.71 million for the last twelve months, virtually flat compared to FY 2024. Looking back a bit, Vistek reported sales of $11.85 million for FY 2023, representing at least 41.4% year-over-year growth for FY 2024.

Even with sales slowing down a bit, margins improved substantially compared to FY 2024. They grew 300 bp for EBITDA and 305 bp for EBIT margin.

Note how this has played out over the past few years:

On top of that, Vistek is already profitable. Net income was $870,000 in FY 2023, growing 29.88% year-over-year to $1.13 million in FY 2024. In the last twelve months, it reached $1.53 million-a net margin of 9.16%.

Even with the challenges of rising receivables and the need to front payments to contractors, Vistek has still managed to keep its operating cash flow in the green. On top of that, its capex needs are minimal - which means free cash flow stays strong.

It's worth noting that over the past two years-and continuing through the last twelve months-Vistek has been steadily deleveraging its balance sheet.

And despite that, the company has consistently maintained a cash surplus! They have more cash on hand than long-term debt.

So I don't see any issues with their balance sheet except for the amount of short-term debt. It's $4.53 million in FY 2024, and it shows that their ability to self-finance their working capital is limited. But that should resolve itself after the IPO.

So, we're looking at a healthy company-no doubt about that. Sales have been growing at double-digit rates year after year, and margins have seen solid improvement.

But even with all that progress, Vistek still has some gaps when it comes to working capital. That's something the IPO should help resolve. But reaching L6 status is still in the "possible, not promised" category.

It all comes down to how wisely they deploy the funds raised-and whether they can prove themselves on the big-ticket Singapore contracts.

Either way, it seems like a much better choice than the companies I've been following that have recently IPOed.

But is it worth the price?

Valuation: An IPO to Remember or Forget?

Let's recall the details.

Vistek will offer 2,250,000 shares at a price range of $4 to $6. This means they are aiming to raise $9 million to $13.5 million, about $11.25 million at their mid-point. For some extra context, once the deal closes, Vistek will have about 21.83 million shares outstanding, giving it an EV of $112.83 million.

That said, let's use the Comps Model.

Based on my estimates, Vistek could report net income of $1.6 million based on 41% year-over-year EPS growth in line with historical EPS growth plus a premium based on recent margin improvement. This would represent an EPS of $0.07.

With the offered price range of $4 to $6, we would have a P/E ranging from 57.14x to 85.71x. Adjusting for 41% year-over-year EPS growth, we would have a PEG ranging from 1.39x to 2.09x.

So, based on the PEG ratio, a fair price for Vistek comes out to around $3 per share-roughly 25% below the low end of the IPO range. That suggests it might be worth keeping an eye on for a potential entry into the secondary market, once the initial buzz settles.

Now looking at EV multiples, in the last twelve months EBITDA has grown by at least 25%. Assuming a slightly higher growth - perhaps 30% year-over-year - we would reach an EBITDA of approximately $2.5 million.

That is an EV/EBITDA of 45.13x. Again, it seems quite high to me, but it is not at stratospheric levels as we see in some IPOs out there.

To give you an idea, Vistek's EV/EBITDA is about 359% of the industry median!

It is also well above other cable companies around the world such as Furukawa Electric (OTCPK:FUWAY) (7.64x) from Japan, Sumitomo Electric (OTCPK:SMTOF) (4.83x) from South Korea, Nexans (OTCPK:NXPRF) (5.28x) from France and others.

With all of this on the table, it looks like Vistek is heading in the right direction. That said, paying $4 per share feels a bit rich. As you probably know, buying into an IPO is rarely a bargain-and this case is no exception.

Still, based on Vistek's growth potential, especially if it manages to secure that L6 license, I'd say an attractive entry point would be somewhere in the $2.50 to $3 range. In this scenario, you would be buying at a PEG <1x. And since cable infrastructure companies don't typically command high valuation multiples, the lower the PEG, the better your margin of safety.

That said, my rating on Vistek is a 'Hold'. I'd recommend staying out of the IPO, and if the price softens post-listing, consider building a position around $3 or below. That's where the risk-reward starts to make a lot more sense.

data.title: Implications For Investors As China Responds To U.S. Trade Policy
data.description:
What are the implications for investors as China responds to the U.S. tariff policy? MoneyTalk’s Greg Bonnell discusses with Haining Zha, Vice President and Director, Asset Allocation Research with TD Asset Management.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell: The markets are volatile as China responds to a fresh round of tariffs enacted by the United States. Joining us now to discuss the investing implications and outlook for the world's second-largest economy is Haining Zha, VPN director for asset allocation research with TD Asset Management.

Haining, thanks so much for joining us. There's a lot going on.

Haining Zha: Yes, certainly. Thanks for having me.

Greg Bonnell: So obviously, we know that we had the big tariff announcement from the Trump administration, the China reaction. We have the United States just moments ago saying that they plan to retaliate, and with even firmer hand. And we're seeing a little tit-for-tat right now. What's actually happening here? And how could this affect these economies?

Haining Zha: So, clearly, things are bad. So the question is, how bad is it? So in terms of tariffs' impact on the GDP, you can actually calculate it two different ways. One, essentially, is treating tariff as a type of tax or cost and then calculate as a percentage of GDP. As we know, China's-- have-- in terms of export to the US, that's about 3 percentage of GDP. And if you account for the 54% cumulative tariff, that's about 1.5 percentage of GDP impact to China.

So, in comparison, if you look at some of the other trading partners, some of them are not necessarily smaller. For example, if you follow the same calculation looking at Korea, it's probably the same impact as a percent of GDP-- is about 1.5%. Japan and the EU is a bit smaller because they are-- they diversify their exports. So by doing the same calculation, you probably get around half of the impact as China.

But some of the other Asian countries, like Vietnam-- if you do the same calculation, that's about 10% of GDP. That's a huge hit. Similar for Taiwan, another trading partner. So their impact is double the amount of China. And on the other end, if you calculate-- doing the same calculation on the US, if you treat tariff as a type of tax, if you are going to collect about $750 billion US additional tariff, that's about 2.5% of GDP. So, you can see that number is actually not small.

Greg Bonnell: As we start the week, the temperature between the two world's largest economies doesn't seem to be coming down, President Trump threatening new 50% tariffs on China if Beijing doesn't remove the retaliatory duties off of last week's announcement. How do we expect this to keep playing out? They are not playing friendly at the moment.

Haining Zha: Right. I think for now, this tit-for-tat retaliation probably will continue for a little while. But I think both sides probably wanted some-- leave some room for negotiation, because in the end, when you step onto [the] negotiation table, your goal is not to do mutual damage. You want to achieve something. So, for example, Trump want to do a deal on TikTok. Trump is a deal person.

Same thing for China. If US approaches China the right way, not threatening retaliation, more retaliation, I think China would be-- love to negotiate. So, I think after all of these tit-for-tat for a while, both sides eventually will find way to de-escalate the whole thing.

Greg Bonnell: You talked about some of the economic damage while they try to find that common ground to have a discussion. Well, we're not there yet. From China's point of view, what can they do to start offsetting that hit to GDP?

Haining Zha: Right. First of all-- so, when I do that calculation, I purely focus on export. But typically what happens is when export go down, the import also goes down. And therefore, the impact on the GDP is naturally smaller. For example, among all of the Chinese export, around 25% is processing trade. Processing trade means you receive goods from other countries, you process it, and then you sell it back to other countries. So, when the export going down, import naturally goes down, probably the same amount.

And then beyond that, you actually-- looking at fiscal deficit this year, China is penciled in a 1% more fiscal deficit spending. So that's a natural offset. And beyond that, I think you could expect further fiscal stimulus announcement from China. I think China is preparing for this worst-case scenario.

Greg Bonnell: How big can we expect the fiscal package to be? I just think of the China narrative-- and you and I have discussed it over the past couple of years-- of investors waiting for that big one. And the big one didn't really come. Could this be the big bit of stimulus from China?

Haining Zha: I think the market whisper number is around 2 trillion RMB. So, if you calculate that 2 trillion, that's about 1.5 percentage of GDP. And we just did that tariff impact calculation. 1.5% is basically, if not entirely offset-- is offset 80%, 90% of whatever impact is.

And if you look at the commentary from the Two Sessions on the National People's Congress-- Two Sessions this year, they are reserving quite a bit of fiscal room and maintain that flexible tone in terms of fiscal stimulus. I think this is the moment they want to use that.

Greg Bonnell: How about the domestic investor in China? We talk a lot about government supports for the different sectors and the strains they've had in that economy. Is the domestic investor in a better position to put money to work in that market?

Haining Zha: I think to understand that, you have to understand that the fundamental setup for the Chinese economy is better than several years before. For example, the key progress is innovation across the board, which gives Chinese investor more confidence. And also, if you look at the real estate market-- the real estate market has been in a downturn for three years in a row. But earlier this year, when we look at the real estate transaction data in terms of floor space sold, it is actually flat year-to-date compared to down 20% or 30% last year.

Right now, that impact on the investment is still not so apparent. But down the road, if you are expecting the markets [to] stabilize here, last year in terms of GDP drag on-- from real estate investment, that's about one percentage point of GDP. So, if you get that back to zero, simply staying flat, that is additional 1% support from there, not to mention the government could do more fiscal spending.

Greg Bonnell: I think it was only several weeks ago you and I had a conversation about the investing opportunity in China, of course, before all of this news of just the past week. Everything changes so fast. Longer term, are you still looking at China as an opportunity market?

Haining Zha: Yeah, we think so. If history is any reference, when you have several days of big down market and if you get in the market at that time, your three-months, six-months, one-year, three-year return from that point on will not be bad, actually. And I think specifically for China, you have to focus on the innovation story, because that is the key change this year.

That innovation, no matter it's from semiconductor or from AI or from robot or people are talking about, say, nuclear reactor or quantum computer-- almost in every category, you can find that China recently had made progress. And in some cases, it's matching the world best performance. So I think that will be a bright spot, particularly after all of this tariff uncertainty goes away. That will be the-- again become the focal point of the investor.
